coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent probes based on N-CQDs: For direct detection of food additives STPP and Al3+
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jianghua
Li, Qing
Wang, Weijie
Wang, Qian
Fu, Yingqiang
coredata.dc:description: Sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP), as one of the many food additives, can cause gastrointestinal discomfort and a variety of adverse reactions when ingested by the human body, which is a great potential threat to human health. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a fast, sensitive and simple method to detect STPP in food. In this study, we synthesized a kind of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), and were surprised to find that the addition of STPP led to the gradual enhancement of the emission peaks of the N-CQDs, with a good linearity in the range of 0.067–1.96 μM and a low detection limit as low as 0.024 μM. Up to now, there is no report on the use of carbon quantum dots for the direct detection of STPP. Meanwhile, we found that the addition of Al3+ effectively bursts the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs@STPP solution and has a good linear relationship in the range of 0.33–6.25 μM with a lower detection limit of 0.24 μM. To this end, we developed a fluorescent probe to detect STPP and Al3+. In addition, the probe was successfully applied to the detection of bread samples, which has great potential for practical application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Sodium tripolyphosphate
Al3+
Fluorescent probe
Food additive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The detection of STPP and Al3+
Detection of Al3+ by N-CQDs@STPP system
Mechanism of action of STPP detection by N-CQDs
Mechanism of fluorescence recovery of N-CQDs after Al3+ addition

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (b) Transmission electron microscopy images of N-CQDs; (c) High-resolution transmission electron microscopy images of N-CQDs; (d) Particle size distribution of N-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs; (b) XRD spectra of N-CQDs; (c) UV spectra of N-CQDs; (d) Raman spectra of N-CQDs.
(a) Full-spectrum scanning XPS of N-CQDs; (b) high-resolution and fitted O1S spectra; (c) high-resolution and fitted N1S spectra; (d) high-resolution and fitted C1S spectra.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs solution after addition of different concentrations of STPP; (b) linear relationship between fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs and STPP concentration; (c) effect of different food additives on the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs; (d) fluorescence emission spectra of different concentrations of Al3+ added to N-CQDs@STPP system; (e) linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and STPP concentration of N- CQDs@STPP system linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Al3+ concentration; (f) specific selection of N-CQDs@STPP system for Al3+.

(a) Comparison of fluorescence lifetimes of N-CQDs alone and the N-CQDs@STPP system with the addition of STPP and its fitting; (b) IR spectra of N-CQDs, STPP and N-CQDs@STPP; (c) Comparison of fluorescence lifetimes of N-CQDs@STPP and N-CQDs@STPP/Al3+ systems and their fitting situation; (d) Zeta potentials of N-CQDs, N-CQDs@STPP, Al3+, and N-CQDs@STPP/Al3+.
(a) and (b) AFM images of N-CQDs solution. (c) and (d) AFM images of the N-CQDs@STPP system after the addition of STPP.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent probes based on N-CQDs: For direct detection of food additives STPP and Al3+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP), as one of the many food additives, can cause gastrointestinal discomfort and a variety of adverse reactions when ingested by the human body, which is a great potential threat to human health. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a fast, sensitive and simple method to detect STPP in food. In this study, we synthesized a kind of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), and were surprised to find that the addition of STPP led to the gradual enhancement of the emission peaks of the N-CQDs, with a good linearity in the range of 0.067–1.96 μM and a low detection limit as low as 0.024 μM. Up to now, there is no report on the use of carbon quantum dots for the direct detection of STPP. Meanwhile, we found that the addition of Al3+ effectively bursts the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs@STPP solution and has a good linear relationship in the range of 0.33–6.25 μM with a lower detection limit of 0.24 μM. To this end, we developed a fluorescent probe to detect STPP and Al3+. In addition, the probe was successfully applied to the detection of bread samples, which has great potential for practical application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As contemporary science and technology continue to advance, the types and quantities of food additives are also increasing, bringing great changes to the food industry. At the same time, the application of food additives faces a number of challenges; the safety of some additives has not been adequately demonstrated, while the amount of some additives used is controversial. Therefore, testing of various food additives is very important.

Sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP) is a common food additive, which is an amorphous water-soluble linear polyphosphate. It can be used in food as moisture retention agent, quality improver and pH adjuster, etc. It can also achieve certain effect of enhancing the taste of food. However, if used in excess or accumulated in the body, it can affect calcium metabolism in the human body [24]. This is due to the fact that STPP can combine with calcium in food to form calcium phosphate salts that cannot be absorbed by the body, affecting the body's absorption and utilization of calcium, which in turn leads to problems such as calcium deficiency and reduced bone mineral density. Secondly, prolonged excessive intake of STPP may lead to deposition of calcium phosphate on the blood vessel wall and aggravate vascular calcification, thereby increasing the risk of cardiovascular disease, and may also cause gastrointestinal discomfort, resulting in abdominal pain, diarrhea and nausea [19]. However, STPP has a wide range of applications in everyday food products, where it can be used in foods such as bread, biscuits, fruits, and meat products [27]. Based on this, it is important to design a fast, sensitive, and convenient method for detecting STPP in food. currently, the methods known to be utilized for the detection of STPP include capillary electrophoresis [9], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry [25], ion chromatography [2], nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy [8], rapid chemiluminescence [5], spectrophotometry, paper chromatography [15], and volumetric methods, etc., and although all of them can be used to detect STPP, they all have their own drawbacks. For example, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy is cumbersome and requires more analytical equipment. Paper chromatography and volumetric methods are less accurate and less stable, while spectrophotometry is less sensitive and susceptible to co-existing substances. Thus, the development of an easy-to-use, sensitive, affordable, and ecologically friendly STPP detection technology is imperative.

Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles smaller than 10 nm are called Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs). It was first discovered by Xu et al. [26] during the isolation and purification of carbon nanotubes and was named by Sun et al. [20]. Since then, the researchers have delved into the different synthesis methods, properties, and applications of CQDs. Carbon quantum dots have the advantages of simple synthesis, abundant sources, good dispersion, easy functionalization, low toxicity, high biocompatibility and controllable optical properties [16]. This has led to its gradual emergence as the most sought-after fluorescent marker and has been extensively studied in many fields, such as photocatalysis [7], bio-imaging [10], drug delivery [12], electrocatalysis [21], and so on. Carbon Dots in Biomedical, Fluorescence Imaging [22], heavy metal detection, cancer treatment, and water pollution treatment [18].

In recent years, fluorescence method has been favored by the public for its advantages of high sensitivity, strong selectivity, real-time monitoring, simple operation and green environment. Before the detection of STPP in this study, there were very few studies on the detection of STPP using fluorescence method. Zheng et al. synthesized N-CQDs using citric acid and ethylenediamine, and constructed a ratiometric fluorescent probe by combining them with calcineurin. The fluorescence signal of this probe decreases after the addition of Eu3+, but gradually recovers after the addition of STPP, thus realizing the indirect detection of STPP [27]. Currently, no report has been found on the direct detection of STPP using carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes.

In this paper, Citric acid and N, N-dimethylformamide were used to produce N-CQDs in a single step. Based on the N-CQDs, a highly sensitive fluorescent probe was constructed for detecting food additive STPP, with the lowest detection limit of 13.02 nM. This is the first report that We've found of using fluorescent carbon dots to directly detect the food ingredient STPP. Meanwhile, the N-CQDs@STPP systemcan detect Al3+ concentrations as low as 0.24 μM. Based on this, in this study, A fluorescent probe with a “On-On-Off” feature was developed to detect STPP and Al3+, respectively, and the method has high sensitivity and selectivity for both substances.

Citric acid (CA), sodium tripolyphosphate (STPP), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), aluminum nitrate, aspartame (ASP), sodium dehydroacetate (NADH), neotame (NEO), sodium thiocyanate (STC), cyclamate (CYC), stevia glycoside (SG), sodium metabisulphite (SMB), acesulfame (ACE), melamine (MA), sucralose (SUC, Borax (Bx), Sunset Yellow (SY), Ascorbic Acid (AA), Potassium Sorbate (PS), Curcumin (CM), Quinoline Yellow (QY), Lemon Yellow (TZ), Malachite Green (MG), Potassium Fluoride (KF), Potassium Hydrogen Phosphate (KH2PO4), Copper Chloride (CuCl2), Barium Hydroxide (Ba(OH)2), sodium bicarbonate (SBC, NaHCO3), Sodium Carbonate (Na2CO3), potassium bromide (KBr), sodium nitrite (NaNO2), and zinc acetate Zn (CH3COO)2 were purchased from McLean Biochemicals (Shanghai, China) with Analytical pure.

Themodelsandmanufacturersofinstrumentsusedinthiswork,includingUVvisiblespectrophotometers,fluorescencespectrometers,Fouriertransforminfraredspectrometers,high-resolutiontransmissionelectronmicroscopes,X-rayphotoelectronspectroscopy,transientstatefluorescencespectrometers,andcentrifuges,areconsistentwiththoseourpreviouslyreported [23].

The N-CQDs synthesis was made with minor adjustments based on a previous report [13]. The N-CQDs were synthesized utilizing a one-step hydrothermal technique that used citric acid as a carbon source and DMF for nitrogen source. Specifically, 2 g of citric acid was dissolved with 10 mL DMF in 25 mL of water, stirred with a magnetic stirrer until dissolved, and then transferred to a reactor for reaction. After completion of the reaction, after letting the reactor cool to ambient temperature, the resultant solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1500 Da) for 72 h to eliminate impurity substance, and it was then centrifuged for 20 min at 15,000 rpm. Finally, vacuum freeze-drying the treated samples yielded black carbon quantum dots solids. It was stored at 4 °C for backup.

There are certain differences in the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs synthesized under different synthesis conditions. Therefore, the effects of various experimental variables on the fluorescence intensity of synthesized N-CQD were studied, including pH, reaction time, reaction temperature, and storage time, etc., prior to the detection of STPP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of STPP and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement and recovery, synthesized N-CQDs were applied for the detection of STPP and Al3+. Firstly, 1 mL of N-CQDs solution (0.15 mg‧L−1) was prepared, and then different concentrations of STPP (0–100 μM) solution were added separately at room temperature, and then dilute it to 3 mL with deionized water, and then stir and mix it evenly before measuring the fluorescence spectra, and the excitation wavelength was 466 nm. Next, the fluorescence spectra were collected after mixing different amounts of Al3+ (0–100 μM) into the N-CQDs@STPP system. Measurement spectral range: 480 nm–650 nm.

To test the selectivity of this method, a range of popular food additives (10−2 mol‧L−1) for STPP were substituted under identical assay conditions. To evaluate the ability of N-CQDs to resist interference, we also introduced a variety of common anions and cations into the N-CQDs@STPP system.

Sample bread was purchased from a local supermarket (Anhui, China), and bread samples were processed according to previously reported procedures in order to avoid matrix interference in food samples [4]. A 0.25 μm filter membrane was used to filter the supernatant after 2 g of dried bread was ground into powder and combined with 20 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was then sonicated for 5 min, centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm‧min−1, and finally collected. After being diluted with deionized water 10-fold, the recovered filtrate was kept at 4 °C. Under ideal test circumstances, the level of STPP in bread samples was ascertained using the conventional additive method.

After preliminary experimental exploration, the time for synthesizing N-CQDs was determined to be 10 h and the temperature was 200 ℃, as demonstrated in Fig. S1. The fluorescence quantum yield of N-CQDs synthesized under this condition was 6.85 %. The synthesized N-CQDs were characterized and TEM and HR-TEM were used to observe their morphology. As shown in Fig. 1 (a, b), the carbon quantum dots exhibit well-dispersed spherical or spheroidal carbon nanoparticles. Fig. 1(c) demonstrates that the lattice spacing of the N-CQDs is 0.212 nm. By statistically analyzing the 200 carbon nanoparticles in Fig. 1(b), it is concluded that the average particle size of the N-CQDs is about 3.5 nm, as shown in Fig. 1(d).

The functional groups of N-CQDs were analyzed by IR spectrograms as shown in Fig. 2 (a). In the IR spectrograms of the N-CQDs, it was observed that the OH/NH stretching vibration was located at 3450 cm−1, the CO/CN stretching vibration was located at 1630 cm−1, the characteristic peak of the CH stretching vibration appeared at 1390 cm−1, and the absorption peak of the CN/CO stretching vibration was located at 1080 cm−1.Fig. 2(b) shows the X-ray diffractogram (XRD) of N-CQDs. The figure shows a diffraction peak centered at 2θ = (26.5°), corresponding to the (002) face of the graphite crystal, which suggests a graphite-like structure in the N-CQDs [3]. The UV absorption spectra of N-CQDs are displayed in Fig. 2(c). The two broad peaks at 1346 cm−1 and 1573 cm−1, which correspond to the D and G peaks, respectively, can be detected in the Raman spectra of N-CQDs in Fig. 2(d). The ratio of the two peaks (ID/IG) is 0.86 < 1, which indicates that the carbon component is well graphitized in the N-CQDs. Raman results confirmed the presence of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon in the N-CQDs, corresponding to the above XRD results [11].

The elemental compositions of N-CQDs were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique as shown in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3(a), the XPS spectrum shows three distinct peaks located at 285.2 eV, 400.3 eV and 532.5 eV, representing the C1s, N1s and O1s spectra, respectively, indicating that the N-CQDs are mainly composed of the elements C, N and O. The XPS spectra of the N-CQDs are shown in Fig. 3(b). Then, the high-resolution XPS of C1s, N1s and O1s were further analyzed. In Fig. 3(b), the high-resolution spectrum of the C1s peak showed two major peaks located at 284.8 eV and 286.2 eV, corresponding to the CO and CN groups, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 3(c), the high-resolution spectra of the O1s peaks showed two separate peaks at 531.2 eV and 532.0 eV attributable to CO and COH groups. Fig. 3(d) demonstrate a high-resolution spectrogram of N1s, which shows three characteristic peaks at 397.2, 400.2, and 401.3, which are attributed to CN, Amine/Amide, and Pyridine-N-oxide functional groups [17]. These analyses indicate the success of N elemental doping and are consistent with the analyses of the IR spectrograms described above.

As illustrated in Fig. S2(a), the excitation and emission wavelengths of N-CQDs are 466 nm and 550 nm, respectively. The emission spectra of N-CQDs at various excitation wavelengths are displayed in Fig. S2(b). The red-shift in N-CQDs' greatest emission peak position with a change in excitation wavelength is shown in the figure, suggesting that the N-CQDs exhibit excitation dependence. The emission spectra of N-CQDs at various temperatures and with 466 nm excitation light are displayed in Fig. S2(c). It can be seen that Upon increasing the test temperature from 20 °C to 240 °C, it is evident that the N-CQDs' emission peaks' position, shape, and intensity did not significantly alter, which suggests that the N-CQDs' emission signals are independent of temperature, suggesting that the N-CQDs have a good thermal stability [6]. To assess the photostability of N-CQDs, they were irradiated under a xenon lamp for varying intervals to evaluate the change in fluorescence intensity, as depicted in Fig. S2(d). Evidently, excellent photostability is shown by the fact that the N-CQDs' fluorescence intensity did not fluctuate noticeably over the course of the irradiation time.

Prior to the detection of STPP, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was firstly investigated under different pH conditions, and the specific results are shown in Fig. S3. According to the figure, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was almost unchanged under different pH conditions, which indicated that the carbon quantum dots had a very good tolerance to pH, and then there was no need to adjust the pH value in the subsequent experiments. Secondly, in order to evaluate the stability of N-CQDs, the effect of different storage time on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. S4(a). It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs hardly changes with the increase of storage time, and its indicates that the carbon dots have good stability. The effect of ion concentration on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs was explored by adding different concentrations of NaCl to the N-CQDs solution and the results are shown in Fig. S4(b). It was discovered that the ionic concentration had no effect on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs, suggesting that a minimal number of ionizing groups may be present on their surface.

It was discovered that the system's fluorescence intensity is displayed in Fig. 4 (a) following the addition of various concentrations of STPP solution to the N-CQDs solution. It is discovered that as the concentration of STPP in the solution increases, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs at 550 nm gradually increases. In this process, the fluorescence excitation wavelength was fixed at 466 nm for each measurement. In addition, it was calculated that the intensity ratio of the fluorescence emission peaks before and after the addition of STPP had a good linear relationship with the concentration of STPP, as shown in Fig. 4(b). The linear regression equation obtained by linear fitting is:
(1)
F
F 0
= 1.049 + 0.1679
C
STPP

The correlation coefficient R2 is 0.9995, and the concentration (μM) of STPP is represented by the symbol CSTPP. The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs is denoted by F, whereas the initial fluorescence intensity is represented by F0 .

The following formula was used to get the detection lower limit of the detection method:
(2)
L O D = 3 δ / K
where k is the linear regression equation's slope and δ is the blank sample's standard deviation, 0.067–1.96 μM was the linear range, and the lowest detection limit was calculated to be 0.024 μM. Table S1 displays the results of a comparison with other reported STPP assays.

To better explore N-CQDs' selectivity for STPP and examine the N-CQDs@STPP system's resilience to interference when other food additives are present. In this study, a variety of (ASP, NADH, NEO, STC, CYC, SG, SMB, ACE, MA, SUC, Bx, SY, AA, PS, CM, QY, TZ, MG) different food additives were selected and tested and the results are shown in Fig. 4(c). It was observed that only the addition of STPP significantly enhanced the fluorescence of N-CQDs, while the addition of other food additives hardly affected the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. This suggests that these N-CQDs have high selectivity for STPP. Furthermore, the figure illustrates how the presence of other food additives little affects the detection of STPP, suggesting that the anti-interference ability of the N-CQDs@STPP system is good.

So far, the detection of STPP in food additives has rarely been reported. In this study, the fluorescent probe constructed by N-CQDs was used for the first time to directly detect STPP, which has the advantages of simplicity, sensitivity, and environmental friendliness compared with other detection methods, suggesting that this method has a great potential for future application in the detection of STPP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Al3+ by N-CQDs@STPP system
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4(d) displays the system's fluorescence intensity after adding different levels of Al3+ solution to the N-CQDs@STPP solution. The results in the figure show that the fluorescence intensity of the N-CQDs@STPP system gradually decreases with the addition of Al3+. Fig. 4(e) shows a good linear relationship between the ratio of F to F0 and Al3+. The linear regression equation obtained by linear fitting is:
（3）
F
F 0
= 0.9759 - 0.05525
C
A l
3 +

In this equation, F0 represents the starting fluorescence intensity value of the N-CQDs@STPP system, whereas F represents the fluorescence intensity of the system after the addition of Al3+, and CAl 3+ denotes the concentration of Al3+ (μM), and the correlation coefficient between them is R2 = 0.9978. Based on the above method, the detection limit of the assay was calculated to be 0.24 μM, and the linear range was 0.33–6.25 μM. Comparisons were made with other published methods for Al3+ detection and the results are shown in Table S2.

Subsequently, to examine the N-CQDs@STPP system's selectivity towards Al3+ and the system's overall anti-interference capability, 17 different anions and cations were selected for testing in this study, as shown in Fig. 4(f). The results in the figure show that only the addition of Al3+ can significantly burst the fluorescence of the N-CQDs@STPP system, and the addition of other ions has almost no effect on it, It suggests that the selectivity of the N-CQDs@STPP system towards Al3+ is good. In addition, It was found that the total system of N-CQDs@STPP/Al3+ was virtually unaffected by the addition of various anions and cations, It shows that the N-CQDs@STPP/Al3+ system has excellent anti-interference capabilities, and demonstrates the potential use of the N-CQDs@STPP system for Al3+ detection in real-world settings.

To verify that the fluorescent probes developed in this work are suitable for practical applications, STPP in bread samples was examined. The results showed that the concentration of STPP in bread samples purchased from a local supermarket was 0.05 μM. Furthermore, Recovery experiments were also carried out and the recoveries of STPP were calculated from 97.67 % to 102.0 % based on the standard additive recovery method, with an average recovery of 100.2 %, as shown in Table S3. These results show that the fluorescent probe created in this work has a great deal of potential for use in food analysis and can be utilized to detect STPP in bread samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of action of STPP detection by N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S5(a–d) shows the transmission electron microscopy images of N-CQDs before and after the addition of STPP, and it can be seen that the addition of STPP led to a significant aggregation of N-CQDs. To explore the underlying reason, we measured the system's fluorescence lifetime before and after adding STPP, respectively, The outcomes are displayed in Fig. 5 (a). N-CQDs have a fluorescence lifespan of 6.62 ns, while the N-CQDs@STPP system has a lifetime of 6.27 ns, with almost no change occurring. It can be concluded that STPP is interacting with the groups on the surface of the ground state N-CQDs thereby not changing their fluorescence lifetime. Fig. 5(b) shows the infrared spectra of N-CQDs, STPP, and N-CQDs@STPP solution, from which it can be seen that after STPP was added to the N-CQDs solution, the POC bond at 900 cm−1 and the PO bond at 1140 cm−1 of STPP disappeared, which indicated that the functional groups on the surface of STPP and N-CQDs had undergone interaction. The distinctive feature of STPP is the large number of oxygen-containing groups in its structure, as shown in Fig. S6. These groups can establish several hydrogen bonds with functional groups on the surface of carbon quantum dots, including CH, OH, and NH. This hydrogen bonding interaction not only firmly connects the STPP and N-CQDs, but also effectively restricts the free vibration and rotation of the molecules on the surface of N-CQDs. Therefore, this intermolecular interaction leads to the aggregation of N-CQDs, which triggers the fluorescence aggregation-induced enhancement phenomenon, which is in agreement with the results exhibited in the IR spectrograms [14]. In order to reveal this phenomenon more comprehensively, AFM was used to take images of the surface microstructure of N-CQDs before and after the addition of STPP, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 (a–d). It is clearly seen that the incorporation of STPP triggered the significant aggregation of N-CQDs. The infrared spectral spectra and the changes in the morphology of N-CQDs further support that STPP is interacting with the surface groups of N-CQDs in the ground state. This observation is consistent with that of transmission electron microscopy, which further strengthens the credibility and reliability of our findings.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence recovery of N-CQDs after Al3+ addition
ce:section.ce:para: It is obvious from Fig. S7(a, b) that with the addition of Al3+ makes the N-CQDs, which had already undergone aggregation, become dispersed again. Fig. 5(c) shows the fluorescence lifetime tests conducted on the N-CQDs@STPP system prior to Al3+ addition and the N-CQDs@STPP/Al3+ system following Al3+ addition. The results show the fluorescence lifetime of 6.27 ns for N-CQDs @ STPP and 6.63 ns for the N-CQDs@STPP/Al3+ system, indicating that the addition of Al3+ disrupts the aggregated state of N-CQDs and is consistent with the results of the TEM images. This data further demonstrates that the addition of Al3+ disrupts the aggregation state of N-CQDs, and it is compatible with the results exhibited in the transmission electron microscopy images. To better illustrate this phenomenon, the Zeta potentials of N-CQDs, N-CQDs@STPP, N-CQDs@STPP/Al3+, and Al3+ were tested in this study, as shown in Fig. 5(d). The results depicted in the figure demonstrate that Al3+ has a strong positive charge and N-CQDs@STPP has a significant negative charge, which indicates a strong electrostatic interaction between the two, suggesting a tendency for Al3+ to approach the N-CQDs@STPP system. When Al3+ ions are close to the N-CQDs@STPP system, stable complexes are formed between STPP and Al3+ due to coordination. This coordination reaction results in a reduction of STPP bound to N-CQDs, leading to a return of N-CQDs to a dispersed state from their original aggregated state [1]. Taken together, the addition of Al3+ disrupts the aggregation state in the N-CQDs, leading to the redispersion in the N-CQDs, which it ultimately causes the fluorescence intensity to gradually decline. Transmission electron microscopy images of both confirm this phenomenon more visually.

In this work, N-CQDs were effectively synthesized using a hydrothermal technique and employed as fluorescent probes to detect STPP and Al3+. It was observed that the incorporation of STPP progressively increased the fluorescence of N-CQDs. Upon further investigation,this resulted from the creation of many hydrogen bonds between the STPP and N-CQD surface groups. Meanwhile, it can be observed that the addition of Al3+ leads to a gradual recovery of the fluorescence of N-CQDs. This study is the first to use carbon dots for the direct detection of STPP in food additives, and the fluorescent “turn-on” carbon dots are more selective and more resistant to interference than conventional fluorescence quenching methods. In addition, the fluorescent probe can be used for the determination of STPP in real bread samples with a good average recovery of 100.2 %. This study offers a dependable scientific method for the quick identification and tracking of food additives, while also broadening the use of carbon dots in the field of food safety.
Ethics approval was not required for this research.

Jianghua Wang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Qing Li: Conceptualization, Investigation. Weijie Wang: Validation. Qian Wang: Data curation. Yingqiang Fu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support of this study by the University Synergy Innovation Program of Anhui Province (GXXT-2020-023), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21406001), the Opening Foundation of Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Functional Coordinated Complexes for Materials Chemistry and Application (LFCCMCA-02), the National College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (202310363068).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125036.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
